query: big o notation javascript interview question

selection.greatFrontendReasonIfOmitted: No exact-topic GreatFrontEnd page was confirmed for this JavaScript prompt, so broader JavaScript question-bank pages would be weaker competitors than topic-direct sources.

competitors.label: Big O Cheat Sheet
competitors.selectionReason: The Big O Cheat Sheet is a widely used direct reference for time and space complexity comparisons.
theirStrengths: It is fast and direct for common complexity classes and data-structure trade-offs.
theirGaps: It is less explicit on the real interview lesson that complexity is one signal among hidden constants, memory cost, and actual bottleneck location.

realWorldUseCases: The common mistake is treating it as the only performance signal and ignoring hidden constants, memory cost, or whether the real bottleneck is somewhere else.
actionableExamples: Always say which complexity you mean.
followUpConfusion: A hash map is O(1) average but can degrade to O(n) worst-case. State which case you are discussing.

realWorldUseCases: The cheat sheet is useful for memorization, but our page is stronger because it explains how to use complexity in real engineering decisions instead of as a lookup table.
actionableExamples: Its tabular examples are strong and quick, so the example criterion stays tied.
followUpConfusion: Our page is more explicit on the likely interview follow-ups: time versus space, average versus worst case, and why Big O does not replace profiling.

actionableExamples: If needed later, add one tiny two-loop versus two-sequential-pass example to create more example distance over the cheat-sheet format.

competitors.winReason: The cheat sheet is great for memorization, but our page is stronger because it teaches how to reason with complexity instead of only listing symbols.

competitors.label: GeeksforGeeks Time Complexity with Simple Examples
competitors.selectionReason: GeeksforGeeks is a topic-direct explainer for time complexity intuition and interview examples.
theirStrengths: It is direct on common complexity classes and beginner-friendly examples.
theirGaps: It is less explicit on the production lesson that hidden constants, memory usage, and real bottlenecks still matter.

realWorldUseCases: Big-O is not a badge for memorizing O(n log n).
actionableExamples: Sequential steps add: O(n) + O(n) -> O(n). - Nested loops multiply: O(n) * O(n) -> O(n^2).
followUpConfusion: Always say which complexity you mean. Example: mergesort is O(n log n) time but O(n) extra space.

realWorldUseCases: GeeksforGeeks is good on fundamentals, but our page is more explicit about how to apply Big O to actual performance discussions instead of reciting classes.
actionableExamples: Its examples are useful and topic-direct, so the example criterion remains tied.
followUpConfusion: Our page is clearer on the likely follow-ups interviewers probe: time versus space, best/average/worst case, and why Big O is not the whole performance story.

actionableExamples: Keep the rules-of-thumb block visible so the page stays as concrete as the example-heavy tutorial references.

competitors.winReason: This tutorial explains complexity well, but our page is stronger because it keeps the answer tied to real performance trade-offs and interview framing.
